title: Satellite Communications
description: Practice identifying satellite communication orbits (GEO, LEO, MEO), VSAT technology, latency characteristics, frequency bands (C, Ku, Ka), and environmental factors like rain fade that affect satellite links.
starterCode:
# SATELLITE COMMUNICATIONS EXERCISE

## Part 1: Satellite Orbit Types

1. GEO stands for _______ Earth Orbit, positioned approximately 35,786 km above Earth.
2. A GEO satellite appears _______ in the sky because it orbits at the same speed as Earth's rotation.
3. GEO satellites provide coverage over a large _______ area, typically about one-third of the Earth's surface.
4. LEO stands for _______ Earth Orbit, with altitudes ranging from 160 to 2,000 km.
5. LEO satellites have much lower _______ than GEO because signals travel shorter distances.
6. LEO constellations require many satellites (hundreds or thousands) to maintain continuous _______ coverage.
7. MEO stands for _______ Earth Orbit, typically at altitudes of 2,000 to 35,786 km.
8. MEO satellites are commonly used for _______ navigation systems like GPS.

## Part 2: Latency and Performance

9. GEO satellite Internet has a typical round-trip latency of approximately _______ milliseconds.
10. The high latency of GEO satellites makes them poorly suited for real-time applications like _______ and online gaming.
11. LEO satellite constellations like Starlink achieve latencies of approximately _______ to 40 milliseconds.
12. Satellite Internet download speeds have improved significantly, with modern services offering _______ to 300 Mbps.
13. The delay caused by the signal traveling to the satellite and back is called propagation _______.
14. TCP performance over GEO satellite links suffers due to the long _______ time for acknowledgments.

## Part 3: VSAT Technology

15. VSAT stands for Very Small Aperture _______.
16. A VSAT terminal typically uses a dish antenna between _______ and 2.4 meters in diameter.
17. VSAT networks use a _______ topology where remote terminals communicate through a central earth station.
18. The central earth station in a VSAT network is called the _______.
19. VSAT is commonly used for connectivity in _______ locations where terrestrial broadband is unavailable.
20. A VSAT _______ link carries data from the remote terminal up to the satellite.

## Part 4: Frequency Bands

21. The three primary satellite frequency bands are C-band, Ku-band, and _______-band.
22. C-band operates at frequencies around _______ to 8 GHz and is more resistant to rain fade.
23. Ku-band operates at frequencies around _______ to 18 GHz and is commonly used for consumer satellite TV.
24. Ka-band operates at frequencies around _______ to 40 GHz and offers higher bandwidth capacity.
25. Higher frequency bands provide more _______ but are more susceptible to atmospheric interference.
26. The _______ band requires the largest dish antennas due to its lower frequency.

## Part 5: Environmental Factors and Challenges

27. Rain _______ is the degradation of satellite signal caused by precipitation absorbing and scattering radio waves.
28. Rain fade affects higher frequency bands like _______ band more severely than lower bands.
29. The line of _______ between the satellite dish and the satellite must be unobstructed for proper signal reception.
30. Solar _______ occurs twice yearly when the sun passes directly behind a GEO satellite, briefly disrupting signals.
31. Satellite Internet requires a clear view of the _______ sky (for GEO) to maintain connectivity.
32. Snow and ice accumulation on the satellite _______ can significantly degrade signal quality.
33. Adaptive coding and _______ (ACM) techniques automatically adjust transmission parameters during adverse weather.
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: Satellite Orbit Types

1. GEO stands for **Geostationary** Earth Orbit, positioned approximately 35,786 km above Earth.
2. A GEO satellite appears **stationary** in the sky because it orbits at the same speed as Earth's rotation.
3. GEO satellites provide coverage over a large **footprint** area, typically about one-third of the Earth's surface.
4. LEO stands for **Low** Earth Orbit, with altitudes ranging from 160 to 2,000 km.
5. LEO satellites have much lower **latency** than GEO because signals travel shorter distances.
6. LEO constellations require many satellites (hundreds or thousands) to maintain continuous **global** coverage.
7. MEO stands for **Medium** Earth Orbit, typically at altitudes of 2,000 to 35,786 km.
8. MEO satellites are commonly used for **satellite** navigation systems like GPS.

## Part 2: Latency and Performance

9. GEO satellite Internet has a typical round-trip latency of approximately **600** milliseconds.
10. The high latency of GEO satellites makes them poorly suited for real-time applications like **VoIP** and online gaming.
11. LEO satellite constellations like Starlink achieve latencies of approximately **20** to 40 milliseconds.
12. Satellite Internet download speeds have improved significantly, with modern services offering **100** to 300 Mbps.
13. The delay caused by the signal traveling to the satellite and back is called propagation **delay**.
14. TCP performance over GEO satellite links suffers due to the long **round-trip** time for acknowledgments.

## Part 3: VSAT Technology

15. VSAT stands for Very Small Aperture **Terminal**.
16. A VSAT terminal typically uses a dish antenna between **0.75** and 2.4 meters in diameter.
17. VSAT networks use a **star** topology where remote terminals communicate through a central earth station.
18. The central earth station in a VSAT network is called the **hub**.
19. VSAT is commonly used for connectivity in **remote** locations where terrestrial broadband is unavailable.
20. A VSAT **uplink** carries data from the remote terminal up to the satellite.

## Part 4: Frequency Bands

21. The three primary satellite frequency bands are C-band, Ku-band, and **Ka**-band.
22. C-band operates at frequencies around **4** to 8 GHz and is more resistant to rain fade.
23. Ku-band operates at frequencies around **12** to 18 GHz and is commonly used for consumer satellite TV.
24. Ka-band operates at frequencies around **26** to 40 GHz and offers higher bandwidth capacity.
25. Higher frequency bands provide more **bandwidth** but are more susceptible to atmospheric interference.
26. The **C** band requires the largest dish antennas due to its lower frequency.

## Part 5: Environmental Factors and Challenges

27. Rain **fade** is the degradation of satellite signal caused by precipitation absorbing and scattering radio waves.
28. Rain fade affects higher frequency bands like **Ka** band more severely than lower bands.
29. The line of **sight** between the satellite dish and the satellite must be unobstructed for proper signal reception.
30. Solar **outage** occurs twice yearly when the sun passes directly behind a GEO satellite, briefly disrupting signals.
31. Satellite Internet requires a clear view of the **southern** sky (for GEO) to maintain connectivity.
32. Snow and ice accumulation on the satellite **dish** can significantly degrade signal quality.
33. Adaptive coding and **modulation** (ACM) techniques automatically adjust transmission parameters during adverse weather.
hints: GEO = Geostationary (35,786 km, appears stationary); LEO = Low (160-2,000 km); MEO = Medium (2,000-35,786 km).
GEO satellite round-trip latency is ~600ms; LEO constellations like Starlink achieve ~20-40ms latency.
LEO constellations need hundreds/thousands of satellites for global coverage; GEO needs only 3 for most of Earth.
VSAT = Very Small Aperture Terminal; uses star topology with a central hub station.
VSAT is ideal for remote locations; the uplink sends data from the terminal to the satellite.
C-band (4-8 GHz) = largest dishes, most rain-resistant; Ku-band (12-18 GHz) = consumer satellite TV.
Ka-band (26-40 GHz) = highest bandwidth but most susceptible to rain fade and atmospheric interference.
Rain fade degrades satellite signals — higher frequencies (Ka-band) are affected most severely.
Line of sight must be unobstructed; GEO dishes point south (in Northern Hemisphere) toward the equator.
Solar outage occurs twice yearly when the sun aligns behind a GEO satellite, briefly disrupting signals.

testCases.description: Should correctly explain identifies ka-band concepts and implementation details
